items.title: XOR needs a hidden layer
items.scenario: Four 2D points: (0,0) and (1,1) are class 0; (0,1) and (1,0) are class 1. No single straight line separates the classes.
items.explanation: A single perceptron cannot represent XOR. An MLP with two hidden ReLU units can learn features such as "both inputs active" vs "exactly one active," then the output neuron combines them into the XOR boundary — four piecewise regions instead of one hyperplane.

items.title: MNIST digit classifier as a three-tier MLP
items.scenario: Flatten each 28×28 image to 784 inputs. Architecture: 784 → 128 (ReLU) → 64 (ReLU) → 10 logits for digits 0–9.
items.explanation: Two hidden layers give the model room to build digit-specific features from pixel combinations before the 10-way output head. This is a canonical dense MLP baseline before trying convolutions.

items.title: Credit-risk tabular model
items.scenario: 40 numeric features (income, debt ratio, payment history encodings) feed an MLP: 40 → 32 (ReLU) → 16 (ReLU) → 1 sigmoid probability of default.
items.explanation: Hidden layers nonlinearly mix raw features — e.g. high debt ratio only matters when income is low — producing a curved decision surface in the 40-dimensional feature space that a linear logistic model cannot match.

items.title: Regression with a hidden bottleneck
items.scenario: Predict house price from 12 engineered features using 12 → 64 (ReLU) → 32 (ReLU) → 1 linear output and MSE loss.
items.explanation: The hidden stack learns nonlinear interactions (bedrooms × neighborhood score) while the final linear output preserves an unbounded real-valued prediction suitable for regression.
